title: Topic 441: Why Gaming Essays Are Popular: Long-Form Content
content:
# Topic 441: Why Gaming Essays Are Popular: Long-Form Content

## ABSTRACT

In an era characterized by fleeting attention spans and the relentless tyranny of the 15-second video, a peculiar phenomenon has emerged from the digital ether: the long-form gaming essay. This paper embarks on a quixotic quest to understand why a generation raised on instant gratification would willingly subject themselves to multi-hour video treatises on the socio-economic implications of the turnip market in *Animal Crossing*. We posit that this trend is not an anomaly but a deep-seated psychological need for intellectual engagement, a rebellion against the snack-sized content that dominates modern media. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of cognitive science, media theory, and the existential dread of finishing a beloved RPG, this paper argues that long-form gaming essays are the brain’s equivalent of a gourmet, slow-cooked meal in a world of digital fast food. They are a testament to the enduring human desire for meaning, narrative, and a really, *really* long-winded explanation of why *Dark Souls* is a metaphor for doing your taxes.

## INTRODUCTION

Behold the modern internet user, a creature of habit, endlessly scrolling through a digital smorgasbord of memes, hot takes, and videos of cats falling off furniture. Their attention span, so the prevailing wisdom goes, is a fragile, fluttering thing, roughly equivalent to that of a goldfish with a particularly bad case of amnesia. And yet, in the dimly lit corners of YouTube and personal blogs, a rebellion is brewing. It is a quiet, contemplative rebellion, one that unfolds not in the streets but in the quiet hum of a computer fan. This is the world of the long-form gaming essay, a genre where three-hour deep dives into the ludonarrative dissonance of *BioShock* are not just tolerated but celebrated.

This paper asks a simple, yet profound, question: what in the name of Hideo Kojima is going on here? Why are millions of people, who can barely sit through a 30-second ad, dedicating entire evenings to what is essentially a university lecture on video games, but with more jokes about glitches and overpowered bosses? Is it a form of digital self-flagellation? A desperate cry for intellectual stimulation in a world that increasingly values brevity over substance? Or is it something more?

We will argue that the popularity of long-form gaming essays is a direct response to the cognitive and emotional vacuity of much of modern online content. These essays, far from being a niche interest for a handful of hardcore gamers, tap into a fundamental human need for deep engagement, narrative cohesion, and the simple joy of watching someone far more articulate than ourselves explain why we cried at the end of *The Last of Us*. This paper will explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the neurological benefits of “deep reading” to the parasocial relationships we form with the essayists themselves. We will journey into the heart of the gaming community, a place where a 45-minute video on the design of a single level in *Super Mario 64* can be a viral sensation. Prepare for a long, strange trip into the psyche of the modern gamer, a journey that will be as insightful as it is, we hope, amusing.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has yet to fully turn its gaze to the specific phenomenon of the long-form gaming essay. However, a wealth of research in cognitive science, media studies, and psychology provides a fertile ground for our investigation. The most relevant body of work concerns the cognitive effects of “deep reading” versus the “shallow reading” encouraged by most online platforms. Maryanne Wolf, in her seminal work *Proust and the Squid: The Story and Science of the Reading Brain*, argues that the digital age is rewiring our neural circuits for speed and multitasking, at the expense of the slower, more immersive cognitive processes required for deep reading. While Wolf’s focus is on text, her insights are directly applicable to the consumption of long-form video essays. These essays, with their complex arguments, detailed analysis, and narrative structure, demand a level of sustained attention that is the very antithesis of the “information-grazing” that characterizes our online lives.

Further support for this idea comes from the field of neuroscience. Studies have shown that engaging with complex narratives, whether in books or on screen, activates multiple brain regions associated with language, memory, and even sensory experience. As reported in *National Geographic*, reading a novel can increase connectivity in the brain for days after the fact. We can extrapolate that a well-crafted gaming essay, which often weaves together narrative analysis, personal anecdote, and historical context, provides a similar, if not more potent, form of cognitive stimulation. The brain, it seems, craves a good story, and a 2-hour video on the lore of *Fallout* is, in its own way, a modern epic.

Of course, we cannot ignore the social and psychological dimensions of this phenomenon. The rise of the YouTube essayist has created a new kind of celebrity: the intellectual gamer. These creators are not just disembodied voices; they are personalities, guides who lead us through the complex worlds of our favorite games. This creates a powerful parasocial relationship, a one-sided bond between creator and viewer that can be a significant driver of engagement. As we will explore in our analysis, the appeal of these essayists is not just what they say, but how they say it, and the sense of community they foster around their work. The existing literature on parasocial relationships, while not specific to gaming, provides a crucial framework for understanding this aspect of the phenomenon. In a world of fleeting connections, the long-form gaming essay offers a sense of sustained, meaningful engagement, both with the content and the creator.

## ANALYSIS

The enduring popularity of long-form gaming essays can be attributed to a confluence of psychological, social, and technological factors. At its core, this phenomenon represents a form of “intellectual comfort food” for a generation starved of deep, meaningful content. In a media landscape dominated by the ephemeral and the superficial, the long-form gaming essay offers a sanctuary of sustained thought and critical engagement. It is a space where complexity is not just tolerated but celebrated, where a deep dive into the moral philosophy of *Deus Ex* is not seen as pretentious but as a worthwhile intellectual pursuit.

One of the key psychological drivers of this trend is the concept of “flow,” a state of complete immersion in an activity, as described by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi. When we are in a state of flow, we are so engrossed in what we are doing that we lose track of time and our sense of self. Gaming itself is a powerful inducer of flow, and long-form gaming essays can be seen as an extension of this experience. By providing a deep, analytical framework for understanding the games we love, these essays allow us to re-enter the world of the game in a new and more meaningful way. They transform our passive consumption of entertainment into an active process of intellectual discovery.

Another crucial factor is the role of the essayist as a _charismatic guide_ and _intellectual companion_. The most successful gaming essayists are not just knowledgeable; they are also engaging storytellers, witty commentators, and relatable personalities. They create a sense of intimacy and connection with their audience, fostering a community of like-minded individuals who share a passion for deep, thoughtful gaming content. This parasocial relationship is a powerful motivator for viewers, who are not just seeking information but also a sense of belonging and intellectual camaraderie.

Finally, we must consider the technological context in which this trend has emerged. The rise of platforms like YouTube and Patreon has made it possible for creators to produce and monetize long-form content, freeing them from the constraints of traditional media. This has created a vibrant ecosystem of independent creators who are able to pursue their passions and connect with a global audience. The long-form gaming essay is a product of this new media landscape, a testament to the power of the internet to foster niche communities and create new forms of cultural expression.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The world of long-form gaming essays is populated by a diverse cast of creators, each with their own unique style and focus. One of the most prominent examples is **Noah Caldwell-Gervais**, whose multi-hour retrospectives on entire game franchises are the stuff of legend. His video on the *Resident Evil* series, for instance, is a sprawling, seven-hour epic that is as much a work of literary criticism as it is a piece of game analysis. Caldwell-Gervais’s work is characterized by its exhaustive research, personal reflections, and a calm, authoritative delivery that makes even the most convoluted lore seem comprehensible.

Another key figure is **Hbomberguy (Harry Brewis)**, whose essays are known for their sharp wit, political commentary, and a healthy dose of righteous indignation. His video “In Defense of Dark Souls 2” is a masterclass in argumentative essay writing, a passionate and hilarious takedown of the game’s detractors. Hbomberguy’s work demonstrates the power of the gaming essay to be not just informative but also persuasive and deeply personal.

We also have creators like **Jacob Geller**, whose essays explore the intersection of gaming with art, philosophy, and history. His video “Humans are Bags of Meat (in Games)” is a haunting and beautiful meditation on the nature of violence and empathy in video games. Geller’s work is a testament to the intellectual and emotional depth that can be found in the medium, and his essays are a powerful argument for the cultural significance of video games.

These are just a few examples of the many talented creators who are pushing the boundaries of the long-form gaming essay. From the detailed design analysis of **Game Maker’s Toolkit (Mark Brown)** to the comedic cultural commentary of **NakeyJakey (Jakey)**, the genre is a vibrant and constantly evolving space. What all of these creators have in common is a deep love for the medium and a commitment to creating thoughtful, engaging content that respects the intelligence of their audience.

## DISCUSSION

The rise of the long-form gaming essay has significant implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits in the digital age. It challenges the prevailing narrative of a generation with a perpetually shrinking attention span, suggesting that the desire for deep, meaningful content is not only alive and well but is, in fact, a powerful market force. This has profound implications for content creators and marketers, who have long been told that the key to success is to be shorter, faster, and more immediately gratifying.

The popularity of these essays also speaks to a growing desire for authenticity and intellectual honesty in our media consumption. In a world of clickbait headlines and algorithmically generated content, the long-form gaming essay feels like a refreshingly human creation. It is a product of passion, expertise, and a genuine desire to share knowledge and connect with others. This suggests that consumers are not just passive recipients of information but are actively seeking out creators who they can trust and with whom they can form a genuine connection.

Furthermore, the success of this genre highlights the changing nature of entertainment and education. The line between the two is becoming increasingly blurred, as creators find new and innovative ways to make learning engaging and fun. The long-form gaming essay is a perfect example of this trend, a form of “edutainment” that is both intellectually stimulating and deeply entertaining. This has significant implications for the future of education, suggesting that the traditional lecture format may be giving way to more dynamic and interactive forms of learning.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, chaotic bazaar of the internet, where attention is the most valuable currency, the long-form gaming essay stands as a defiant monument to the enduring power of deep thought and sustained engagement. It is a testament to the fact that, even in a world of endless distraction, there is still a profound human need for stories, for analysis, and for the simple joy of learning something new. The success of this genre is not an anomaly; it is a sign of a changing media landscape, one where passion, expertise, and a genuine connection with the audience are becoming the new metrics of success.

So, the next time you find yourself three hours deep into a video about the political economy of the Galar region in *Pokémon Sword and Shield*, do not despair. You are not procrastinating; you are participating in a cultural revolution. You are a member of a growing movement of intellectual explorers, a community of deep thinkers who are pushing back against the tide of superficiality and demanding more from their media. And that, we can all agree, is a game worth playing.
references:
[1] Wolf, M. (2007). *Proust and the Squid: The Story and Science of the Reading Brain*. HarperCollins.

[2] National Geographic. (2026, January 16). *Reading books can help you live longer—here’s how*. https://www.nationalgeographic.com/health/article/reading-longevity-brain-health

[3] Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience*. Harper & Row.
